title: Chapter 30: The First Convoy
content: Three days after the Crossroads agreement, the first supply convoy arrived at Blackwood.

It came through a portal that opened in the main quad at dawn — a tear in the violet sky that widened into an archway of cold blue light. Through it emerged a procession of traders: beings of shadow and bone, pulling carts loaded with crates, barrels, and bundles.
At the front walked Kallos. Its four arms were full of manifests.
"First shipment," it announced. "Medical supplies, preserved food, textile materials, and — as requested — twelve kilograms of high-grade chroma dust for your artist."
Roxanne blinked. "I didn't order chroma dust."
"Your friend did. The one with charcoal on her face."
Lena. Of course.
Roxanne smiled. "How much?"
"Five thousand Infinity Notes. At face value."
"Done."
She handed over a stack of notes. Kallos counted them with three hands while the fourth directed the unloading.

The refugees watched from the dormitory windows. They had seen a lot in the past few months — monsters, miracles, a woman who bought the campus with a black card. But a trade convoy, arriving on schedule, with actual supplies? That was new.
That was hope.
The news reached the Exchange within hours.
A cargo manifest from the Deep Paths. Medical supplies. Food. Textiles. All purchased with Infinity Notes. The first breach in the economic cordon.

The Senior Examiner — the one whose ledgers had turned to ash at the campus gate — stood before the Exchange's High Ledger, a vast book that contained every transaction in the Nether-Grid. Its pages were turning on their own, searching for the relevant entry.

"Transaction recorded," the High Ledger said. Its voice was the sound of pages rustling. "Five thousand Infinity Notes exchanged for standard medical supplies. Vendor: Kallos of the Crossroads. Purchaser: Roxanne Vance, Vance Demesne."
"The Crossroads is not within Exchange jurisdiction," the Senior Examiner said.
"Correct."
"Then we cannot block the transaction."
"Correct."
The Senior Examiner's columnar eyes blinked in slow sequence.
"She found a way around the isolation."
"She found a way through the Deep Paths. The isolation was always a surface-level strategy. The Deep Paths were never part of the Exchange's territory."
"Because the Deep Paths are too dangerous to regulate."
"She regulated them anyway. In one evening."
The Senior Examiner turned away from the High Ledger.
"Inform the Council. The isolation strategy has failed."
"It has not failed. It has been circumvented. There is a difference."
"Not one that matters."
The pages rustled in agreement.
At the campus, Roxanne watched the convoy unload.
Frost stood beside her, tracking every crate with its crystalline fingers.
"The Exchange knows," it said. "The High Ledger recorded the transaction within minutes of its completion."
"Good. Let them watch."
"They will escalate."
"Let them."
"The next step will not be economic."
Roxanne turned to look at Frost.
"What will it be?"
"Political. The Council will demand your presence. They cannot isolate you economically, so they will attempt to constrain you through protocol."
"They want me in a room where they can control the conversation."
"Yes."
Roxanne considered this.
"Then I'll go. But I'll bring my own terms."
"You cannot negotiate with Primordials the way you negotiate with traders."
"Watch me."
That evening, Roxanne sat in her office with Silas.
The summons had arrived. A scroll of black parchment, sealed with the Council's mark — a circle divided into seven segments, one for each Seat. It lay unopened on her desk.
"Protocol," Silas said, looking at the scroll. "They want you to appear before the full Council. To explain your actions."
"To justify myself."
"To submit to their authority."
Roxanne picked up the scroll. Turned it over.
"What happens if I don't go?"
"They will consider it an act of defiance. They will escalate further."
"What happens if I go?"
"You will be in a room with seven beings who want to control you. They will use procedure, tradition, and precedent to box you in."
"So I lose either way."
"Unless you change the game."
She looked at him.
"How do I change the game?"
Silas's red eyes met hers.
"You remind them that you hold the original contract. You are not a petitioner before the Council. You are a member of it. The Second Seat. You have the same voting power as the First."
"The First Seat has been running the Council for millennia. Everyone defers to it."
"Everyone defers because no one has challenged it. But you have already challenged the Exchange, opened trade routes through the Deep Paths, and minted your own currency. What is one more precedent?"
Roxanne was quiet for a moment.
Then she broke the seal on the scroll.
Inside, the message was brief:
*The Second Seat is hereby summoned to appear before the Upper Chamber at the next convergence. Attendance is mandatory. Failure to attend will be considered a vacancy of the Seat.*
"Vacancy," she read aloud. "They're threatening to remove me."
"They cannot remove you. The Seat is tied to the bloodline. Only you can vacate it."
"They're bluffing."
"They are testing whether you know the rules."
Roxanne smiled.
"Then I'll show them I know the rules better than they do."
She set the scroll down.
"When is the next convergence?"
"Three days."
"Plenty of time."
"Time for what?"
"To prepare. To plan. To make sure that when I walk into that chamber, I'm not defending myself. I'm setting terms."
Silas looked at her for a long moment.
"You are going to a Primordial Council meeting to set terms."
"I went to the Deep Paths and came back with a trade route in one evening. A Council meeting should be easier."
"You are underestimating them."
"Maybe. But they're underestimating me too."
*End of Chapter 30*
